sections.title: Contributor Instructions
sections.content:
If you have been given access to this document as a contributor, welcome aboard! 

Simply add your content directly to this document. Everything is saved automatically.

Please edit this document directly. Copying/Pasting from Word etc is likely to break things.

  • Read the Google Docs Cheat Sheet

  • Edit the content inside the email section,
  • below Subject 
  • and above the “Best of the Internet” section

  • Multiple people can edit at the same time

  • Headings
  • When editing - make sure to use proper headings for each section
  • Add headings as required - make sure to use the Heading 1 style for each heading (click the drop down that says “Normal text”)
  • Sub headings within your article should be used from Heading 2 downwards as required.
  • Please try to add your “Author Name reports” heading (h5) under your main article heading

  • Images
  • Please do include images in your article - instructions
  • Try to set the width of your image so its no wider than the page

  • Testing
  • You can preview the email layout by pressing the blue M in a circle next to the “to” section, this will create the email.
  • You can also choose to send it to your personal address if you want to test it there.

sections.title: Newsletter Instructions
sections.content:
These instructions are only for the person actually creating/sending the newsletter.

  • Create a copy of the template file and rename it as YYYY-DD, for example 2022–08
  • Right click the template file, select “Make a copy”
  • Right click the newly created copy and select “rename” and set to the edition date in YYYY-DD format

  • Editing - see above

  • Final Preparations
  • Check images are all no wider than the document
  • Apply headings where authors have not
  • Check all articles have the “author name reports” sub heading
  • Insert “front page” image
  • Prompt members to use the form https://accounts.google.com/v3/signin/identifier?continue=https%3A%2F%2Fdocs.google.com%2Fforms%2Fd%2Fe%2F1FAIpQLSe79ojLfz6TsCSMi1Ehm_sg4aDlw4d5RL--yYwzmrV0QsVMIg%2Fviewform&dsh=S-83270531%3A1783500335598577&followup=https%3A%2F%2Fdocs.google.com%2Fforms%2Fd%2Fe%2F1FAIpQLSe79ojLfz6TsCSMi1Ehm_sg4aDlw4d5RL--yYwzmrV0QsVMIg%2Fviewform<mpl=forms&osid=1&passive=1209600&service=wise&flowName=WebLiteSignIn&flowEntry=ServiceLogin&ifkv=AcDsRvz12A1eUwfhcHPTMHpwClCVpf8-Kf0RtJJ65iFqtjHmE5Lywuo9t2HAu1aVC58VUDmVlxemlw
  • Choose an image from the form for the front
  • Insert->Image->Drive and choose the photo
  • Add “other photos submitted” final heading for anything submitted but didn’t make the cover
  • Insert->Image->Drive and select all newly submitted photos but the cover one

  • Sending
  • Press the blue gmail icon to generate an email
  • WARNING chrome might block the popup. Check for an icon on the right hand side of the address bar. You need to ensure popups are not blocked for Google Drive
  • Fill in recipients etc within the email and press send as normal
  • Test send to a private email address first
  • Once happy, send to the membermojo group email address - can only be done from an approved address
  • Address to send to is members.dhpc@membermojo.co.uk
  • Can only be sent by a pre approved email address in membermojo

  • Creating PDF
  • Use a non personal gmail account (eg I have a josephparagliding@gmail.com account for pg related stuff)
  • Clear out the To section of the email form 
  • Open the email preview and select “print”, but choose “print as PDF”
  • Save the PDF file name YYYY-DD-Skywords.pdf
  • Upload the file to this folder (Skywords / EDITIONS / Skywords Archive Public Share PDFs)

To

members.dhpc@membermojo.co.uk

Cc

Bcc

Subject

Skywords - January 2024

Dear [first_name],

Skywords - January 2024

Its a paraglider under water, courtesy of some AI
The newsletter of the Dales Hang gliding and Paragliding Club

Chairman's Chat

Happy New Year.  As the Jet Stream blows us into 2024, I’m pleased to report that the club is in a good position.

At the end of the season, we had 222 members.  That’s the highest number we have seen for at least a decade.  For those of you that didn’t make it to the AGM (222 – 32 = 190), and for those of you that don’t read the forum (222 – 35* = 187), I’m pleased to report that we have a strong team of committee members leading us into 2024.  We don’t know exactly where the next threat to our flying might come from: landowners, horse riders, gamekeepers, wind farm developers, environmental groups, airspace consultants, or something we haven’t anticipated.  Whatever it is, we promise to do our utmost to represent your best interests.

On behalf of you all I’d like to thank Simon Tomlinson who has stepped down, having served as northern sites officer for 7 years; and Shaun Pickard, his southern counterpart, who has served the club for 6 years.  They have been replaced by Tom Shuttleworth and Simon Browning respectively.  But it’s not just those stepping down and those volunteering to replace them that we need to thank.  The full list of committee members is displayed on the website and I’m sure that I don’t need to repeat the advice contained in the BHPA handbook, ‘Buy them a drink’!

Having wined and dined the farmers back in September, and delivered Christmas drinks to them; they seem (mostly) happy.  Our sites have been secured for another year.

The club had a remarkable cross-country season, with Richard Meek winning the coveted National XC league.  Ably assisted by Jake Herbert, Rob Cruickshank and Chris Fountain, the team secured the Dales Club in top spot, almost 1,000 points ahead of the second placed team.  But, as all XC pilots know, it’s a team effort and everybody who marked a thermal or helped with the retrieve can claim credit.  Let’s not forget that novice pilots are the Skygods of the future.

Pete Logan leads our team of willing coaches.  If you’re a novice pilot looking for advice and guidance, then please get in touch.  If it’s just company that you’re after, or a steer on where to go flying, then keep an eye on the club’s WhatsApp group.

Stef Sykes has organised a series of club nights (first Thu of the month, except Jan which is the second Thu) to keep us sane until the better weather arrives.  And why not draw a book or DVD out of the library to while away the winter evenings?  Keep an eye on the website or various social media outlets to find out what’s going on.

The club’s reserve repack is booked for Sun 14 Jan at Ilkley Grammar School.  Places are going quickly (morning session sold out) so if you want to repack your reserve under supervision then book online here.

You’ll soon be getting an invitation to renew your membership.  The club is in a good financial position so, whilst doing our best to protect our savings from the effects of inflation, we’ve been able to maintain subscriptions at their previous level (£25 reduced to £22.50 if you pay promptly).  We hope that you consider that to be good value for money.

We’ve done everything that we can to get you into the finest quality Yorkshire air in 2024: the rest is up to you (and the Jet Stream).  Fly safely.

Martin Baxter

*I’m basing that on a ‘Merry Christmas’ message that I posted on 23 Dec.  It got 37 views (and 2 responses) over a period of about 4 days.  The figure is flawed because it includes anybody that read it twice, or the original post and then either response separately (as I did).

Social, Next Meeting 11th January

Happy New Year

Our first social evening of 2024 falls on the 11th of January (to allow us all to bed in a bit…). As always, we’re in the Horse and Farrier in Otley at 730pm for an 8pm start.

On this day, we are proud to present Mr Richard Meek regaling us with his exploits over the last year which brought him such fine accolades and fitting trophies of achievement. Richard is known not only for his fine flying abilities, but also for his expert films, photographs and storytelling attributes.

Not to be missed.

DHPC Film and Photo Night Feb 1st

And talking of films, photos and not to be missed, please submit your short films (no longer than 15 mins) and photographs (no bigger than 10MB) to me if you’d like to be in the running to win FANTASTIC prizes at our February social meet on Thursday the 1st of Feb.

Film first prize - Club T Shirt (or Annual membership of DHPC)

Photo first prize - Club T Shirt (or Annual membership of DHPC)

Other prizes, too, for second and third places.

Send films and photos to me by Jan 29th at 0900 to be in the running.

Next club nights are:

March 7th with Kacper and his BP Cup exploits

April 4th with Westie on how to win at Hike and Fly

Flight Reports and Articles

Martin Baxter reports

Cloud Cover in Oktas

A few months ago, somebody asked me why my messages to the WhatsApp Flying Plans Group gave my estimate of cloud cover in eighths – e.g. 6/8ths.  He’d wondered if he could use any fraction – say ⅓ or ¾.

The simple answer is that it’s the convention in meteorology to use Oktas (or eighths).  I’ve never questioned the logic, but I suppose that it’s easy, when looking up at the sky, to split it into quarters, and then one more split and you have Oktas.  It’s also convention to use improper fractions, so it’s 4/8ths (or 4 Oktas) rather than ½ cloud cover.

Oktas also play a part in decoding weather reports and forecasts. METARs (METeorological Aerodrome Report) and TAFs (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast) use text terms to describe the amount of cloud at various heights and the decode is as follows:

Text

Abbreviation

Amount in Oktas

Sky Clear

SKC

0

Few

FEW

1 – 2

Scattered

SCT

3 - 4

Broken

BKN

5 – 7

Overcast

OVC

8

For example, if the Leeds/Bradford METAR contained SCT023 BKN050 it would mean 3-4 Okts at 2,300’ and 5-7 Oktas at 5,000’ (both above airfield elevation).

If somebody reports 9 Oktas it means that they can’t observe the sky, normally because of fog or mist, but in my case it might be my duvet!      

Tales from a teabag; porosity matters

Pete D reports

It was an honest hike, but a pleasant one in the warm and picturesque surroundings of the Craig Meagaidh nature reserve – day 3 of our 2023 Scotland adventure.  Upon reaching Na Cnapanan it was noticeable that there was no wind, and what no wind there was had a distinctly northerly feel to it.  This meant that within a minute of stopping the midges found us, and to escape them we would be launching into the lee.

We hiked up further to the southwest faces of Càrn Lath and waited for the persistent cloud shading out launch to disperse.  A few got bored and gambled.  This even paid off in some cases, although not without an epic fight.  Eventually a midge-fart of a breeze came up the face and the half-dozen of us who were ready, or desperate to evade the irritating little invertebrates, threw ourselves off the hill.

It was a buoyant but snotty little mess of a lee-side thermal: difficult to stay in, too close to the terrain for comfort, and too many of us to relax.  Or was it just me?  I felt like I was accelerating and decelerating all the time as I went in and out of the core and having to put in large control movements to keep stable.  Eventually one of these was over the top and the tips peeled back as an anxious Robin flew past in the other direction.  Hands up, catch, straight line away from the mess, recover.  All fine in practice but a nagging feeling continued this time; something was not as it should be?

Two days prior we had been waiting on top of Creag Dubh above Glencoe ski resort with a light northeastly, looking for the sign.  It arrived in the form a cloud building about 500m from launch.  Dougie, Richard and Rosie, being the ones occupying the scant patches of dryish moss between the crags, piled off and I did a rapid half-hearted attempt at laying out my wing and chasing them.  As my feet left the ground, I looked up to see a perfect bowtie on the right side of the wing.  First reaction, fly out straight as the cliffs fell away beneath me; second, embarrassment at such a rushed imbecilic launch in front of another dozen pilots, both beginner and expert; third, danger over now how Pete’ll fix it?

Fifty seconds can be a very long time; ample time to confirm that the stabilo and brakes were not going to sort this mess, and enough to contemplate a bottom landing on the soft ground by the car park, followed by a pack, another £15 chair lift, and twenty-minute hike to take off.  No!  I was not going to lose an hour or more from a rare epic Scottish cross-country day, especially as the other three were now climbing ahead of me under the increasingly good-looking cloud.

I can’t remember consciously waiting until a specific height above ground, but the tracklog says precisely 200m so perhaps I was.  Once the cliffs had fallen away sufficiently, and this seemed to take an age with such a degraded glide angle, I tucked my legs in, braced and buried the righthand brake.  SIV training is all well and good when done over a lake with a world class instructor watching your every move; it turns out to be a slightly different kettle of fish over a remote Scottish mountainside with a mixed bag of bemused pilots wondering how you are going to get out of the fix.  I did however remember Malin’s words to the effect that spins are something that can be unpredictable; I did not want to have to repeat this so went hard on the first attempt.

The right-hand wing peeled back, and as I turned to face the cliffs I had recklessly departed from, I went to catch the dive.  A quick glance up suggested that most of the lineover had cleared, hurrah, and I was left with a little cravat.  However, the next thing I know I am spinning again, but this time unintentionally.  Hands up, let the wing dive properly, notice I am now flying away from the cliffs again and catch.  The remaining cravat was easily fixed by some line jiggling as my heart rate dropped towards normality and I headed out towards the cloud way below the others who were now well established in a climb.  It is at this point that I first heard the radio with someone on launch piping up with “…from zero to hero…”.  It turns out that there had been other, perhaps useful communications prior to this, such as trying a huge big-ear, but I guess I was too focussed.  Unlike two days later, I was able to put this first incident out of my head and continued on an epic flight across Blackwater dam to Loch Treig, back along the Mamores before making a valiant attempt to get back to Glencoe.

So why had my wing attempted to fall out the sky on me twice in three days when I was pretty current, had 450 hours on the wing, and had never previously accidentally stalled or gone parachutal on any wing?  The last piece of this puzzle, although not chronologically speaking, is that I had had a nagging feeling about the wing for a little while, nothing I could put my finger on, and so had popped into The Sick and the Wrong on the way to Scotland.  The numbers from Steve’s porosity meter did not make great reading; whilst the small white bit of central fabric was medium, the six readings on the red fabric either side were all “inappropriate” or fails with the lowest being 7 seconds.  I was advised not to fly it (https://porosimeter.hu/products/porozit-n/).

When the porosity deteriorates on wings it becomes easier for air to flow through the material.  This in turn disrupts the laminar flow on the wing surface, the thing that keeps it in the air, and the stall speed increases; the glider is more likely to enter deep stalls.  Whilst 450 hours should be fine on a modern well looked-after wing, I had purchased this one second hand in the first place so I would guess it had a minimum of another 100 hours on that.

So, there it was, in-flight verification of the porosity readings I was choosing to ignore, a condemned sail.  My advice: this is a good lesson to learn from other people’s experience as opposed to first hand!

Members

Rahul Basu

It's time to start renewing your membership if you want to get the 10% discount . The club operates a fixed membership year and they expire on 1st February 2023. The good news is that the membership fees are staying the same as last year. So normally it's £25 but £22.50 with the 10% Discount for early renewal.

You will be emailed a renewal reminder which will contain a link to the Membership renew page. To renew please sign into your Membership, confirm your details and change where needed and submit your renewal form. You don't need a password (though you can create one if you prefer) and it only takes a minute or two. You will also need to make a payment, you are not renewed until you have both completed the renewal form and also paid the membership fee.

When making your payment separately please Remember to include your BHPA number as a payment reference. It makes it so much easier to reconcile payments with applications.

If you have set up a standing order to pay automatically, please check it is paid by 1st Feb.  You will still need to complete your membership application.

It is a condition of DHPC membership that you also remain a current member of the BHPA, so please remember to also renew your BHPA membership promptly when it becomes due.

I hope you choose to remain a member of the DHPC for another year. Best wishes for a safe and successful flying season!

Safety

Clive Sury reports

Happy New Year!

Many people start the year with resolutions.  So why not have a few New Year paragliding ones?

Here are some ideas:

  • Download the what3words app so that you can share a location anywhere in the world in an emergency.
  • And/ or download the Ordnance Survey app for location and maps
  • Download the BHPA app to have your membership on your phone
  • Complete the Dales personal information record https://www.dhpc.org.uk/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2936 and send to coaching@dhpc.org.uk
  • Attend the Dales Club reserve repack on 14th Jan - register here: https://www.eventbrite.co.uk/e/dales-club-reserve-repack-day-sunday-14th-jan-2024-tickets-758968203327
  • Buy new Karabiners for your harness - noting a recent fatality in Turkey due to a broken Karabiner.  The recommendation is to replace them every 5 years or 500 hours, whichever first.
  • Set improvement goals on specific aspects of flying, e.g., spot landings; fully inflated glider at launch; forward launches; smoother and tighter 360’s
  • Improve core strength with a 2 minute plank each morning
  • Read a book every month or quarter
  • Attend a first aid and/ or coaching course
  • Perfect the Cobra launch technique
  • Upgrade equipment - the list here is endless!

These are just a few suggestions to get one’s mind engaged.  For a resolution to be worthwhile it’s important that it’s relevant to the individual concerned.  Plus, that the reward for achieving it is known and in front of mind.  Therefore, it’s what you consider important and wish to commit to yourself that matters.

The key point here is that this time of year provides an opportunity to reflect on what’s gone well with flying over the past year and to congratulate oneself for achievements made.  Thereafter, identify areas to improve or change and focus on going forward.  A great source of joy in the sport is the continual progression in ability and knowledge that can be achieved and I wish you well in your development over 2024.

Safe flying,

Clive.

Northern Challenge Series (NCS) -  2024

Ed Cleasby updates.

(This is written principally for newer pilots who may be unfamiliar with the NCS)

The 7th year of the Northern Challenge Series (NCS) kicks off on the 1st February.  The 2024 series has been substantially refreshed with new tasks and several new sites including a revised approach to the Hike & Fly set.  I’ll  run through the changes later, but for pilots new to the concept of the NCS it may be useful to briefly revisit the history and rationale underpinning it.

Some background to the NCS

The main purpose of the NCS is pilot development, however,  it also serves as a competition if you wish to approach it that way. It includes a league table with a scoring system with awards given out at year end.  It emerged from my time as Chief Coach, when I realised that coaching days were often hard to coordinate with the weather; they needed a focus to tie in with individual pilot needs and the primary benefit for new pilots  often became networking – getting to know faces and become flying buddies.  The ‘focus’ aspect I addressed using task cards set at differing levels, later that developed into the standing tasks/challenges that enlarged to form the NCS.  My answer to the weather and the ‘herding cats’ problem,  was to make tasks available and accessible across an extended season.  In other words they can be attempted whenever convenience and the weather coincides. The means to host everything is via  the excellent, xcmap, run by Chris Foster. This provides all the task uploads and flight downloads  If you’re in the first few years of your flying career the NCS offers a pathway to accelerated learning and dovetails easily into coaching or self directed activities.   At the other end of the experience spectrum it provides a racing element – in other words -  against the clock.

Why is the NCS good for pilot development?

First of all, there is nothing wrong with wafting around for an hour enjoying the sensation and the scenery.  I have  many years of doing just that– and still do.  But… you won’t learn a great deal – about yourself, your wing, the site, conditions, or that app/instrument on your flightdeck.  Flying any NCS task will challenge you to push the envelope of all those things whilst increasing your knowledge and skills. The tasks are all site based and designed to  increase in difficulty through to the satisfying  ping of achieving goal (60%).  In terms of difficulty they are of modest length and suitable for the average pilot – the time element (40%)  is for the more ambitious and competitive pilots who wish to race them.  In summary

i)                    You develop navigation skills through setting up and flying a task.

ii)                   You gain a deeper understanding of what your app/instrument is capable of and how to set it up.

iii)                 You explore the extended possibilities of a site instead of staying within your comfort zone beats..

iv)                 You push your own flying skills in order to progress through a series of turnpoints.

v)                  Being focused helps avoid negative thoughts and keeps you in a positive frame of mind.

vi)                 It’s both rewarding and fun.

For more details of the Aims, rules, tasks, scoring etc, go to https://www.xcflight.com/northern-challenge-series-2024/   At the time of writing the tasks are set as illustrative downloads only, they should be live for download on  https://www.xcmap.net/   after the New Year.

Changes for 2024

Main series:  There is no change to the number of tasks, which is held at 14 (best eight scoring).  There are 9 new tasks and four new sites. All the remaining GRIDS have gone, I admit to not liking GRID tasks!  However, no GRID or previous task is lost, they have simply reverted back into the relevant club sets. A few of the new tasks are tweaked originals to make them a little easier – others are completely new and designed to get pilots to explore less well flown sites. An example (left) is Nappa, which having flown there a few times over the past two years I feel deserves more attention.

Another slight change of emphasis is to move tasks away from areas where they weren’t seeing any traffic.  Another consideration is if a club supports and encourages the NCS they get tasks - if not they lose them!  

Hike & Fly series:  This remains at six tasks with the best four scoring. To be more inclusive (less daunting) they have been reduced in length and only the ‘iconic’ Dales Three Peaks remains from the previous set. They can now be regarded as ‘day tasks’.  The spread across the Lakes, Dales and Pennines is about equal. Given a reasonable day it’s quite possible to fly over 50%  of a task as they are based around ridges, bowls and good thermal territory  and allow for different wind directions. An interesting example is the Haweswater circuit in the eastern Lakes (right).

In addition to the Dales Three Peaks, DHPC pilots will find they have two new, local H&F tasks to whet their appetite. They may prove useful training for the  X Dales later in the year.

That is a summary of the NCS 2024.  I don’t need to add anything else as everything is covered via the previous links.  

Any questions please direct to me.

Get planning …. it’s FREE!

—--------------------------------------------------------------------

The 2023 awards were recently presented at the J36 Pizza night as follows.

Main series - Geoff Crossley        Hike & Fly   - Tom Hodgkin        Highest placed ENA/B - Elliott Brown

All Our Yesterdays

If you feel the past should be left, quietly swallowed by the mists of time,  then the  article below is not for you - move on.

However, it shows nothing really changes but the names and the gliders - the experience remains the same, the excitement undiminished, the challenge no different …as for the weather I couldn’t possibly comment.

Back in the day when Wings! produced lots of great local articles, the Dales and Lakes were favoured with some many, first encounters with wave, convergence and thermalling xc flights. I’m fortunate to have most of the very early copies dating back into the late seventies. I still enjoy reading them …. partly nostalgia, but also because they still have a raw freshness and still seem relevant today.

Wings!

May 1980

(Apologies for the reproduction quality, you may need to zoom in and put on your best glasses).

Ed Cleasby

Martin Baxter adds…

Great minds think alike.  In a remarkable coincidence (or is it just that the bad weather is keeping us indoors?), I have also been digging through the archives.

As you may have read on the forum, Lee Collinson offered up some old magazines.  Marc Asquith agreed to take them and, in a logistical operation of which Eddie Stobart would be proud, Lee (whilst visiting his brother) delivered three boxes to Pete Logan in Baildon; Pete handed them to me at the club AGM; and I plan to give them to Marc at the BHPA AGM.  No idea what Marc does with them all.

I couldn’t help but flick through a few of the older copies and, to my surprise, I found them quite illuminating.  I scanned a few items that I thought might be of interest to club members, and I’ll include them in future copies of Skywords (if Ed doesn’t beat me to it!).

I found some of the history quite illuminating.  Prior to 1989 the British Hang Gliding Association (BHGA) had its own magazine called ‘Wings!’ and the British Association of Parascending Clubs (BAPC) had a magazine called ‘Skywalker’.  These magazines were merged into ‘Skywings’ in Apr/May 1989.  Despite resistance from some BHGA members about the new fangled sport of paragliding: mostly concerned about safety and the dangers of mixed flying at congested sites, the associations merged to form the BHPA in 1992.

Flicking through some of the old copies of Wings, it’s clear that DHPC members played a significant role in the BHGA, and the evolution of our sport.  Noel Whittall was chairman of the BHGA.  His son, Robbie, became World Champion in hang gliding, and later in paragliding.  Both are honorary club members.  Trevor Birkbeck, Dean Crosby, Ian Currer and Ed Cleasby all played their part in developing the sport into what it is today.

From Skywalker January 1987:                                        and one month later:

                     

CONTENT ENDS AT THE LINE ABOVE HERE

INSERT YOUR CONTENT ABOVE THE “Best of the Internet” HEADING

AS THAT IS INTENDED TO BE THE FINAL SECTION

sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Happy New Year.  As the Jet Stream blows us into 2024, I’m pleased to report that the club is in a good position.

At the end of the season, we had 222 members.  That’s the highest number we have seen for at least a decade.  For those of you that didn’t make it to the AGM (222 – 32 = 190), and for those of you that don’t read the forum (222 – 35* = 187), I’m pleased to report that we have a strong team of committee members leading us into 2024.  We don’t know exactly where the next threat to our flying might come from: landowners, horse riders, gamekeepers, wind farm developers, environmental groups, airspace consultants, or something we haven’t anticipated.  Whatever it is, we promise to do our utmost to represent your best interests.

We’ve done everything that we can to get you into the finest quality Yorkshire air in 2024: the rest is up to you (and the Jet Stream).  Fly safely.

Martin Baxter

*I’m basing that on a ‘Merry Christmas’ message that I posted on 23 Dec.  It got 37 views (and 2 responses) over a period of about 4 days.  The figure is flawed because it includes anybody that read it twice, or the original post and then either response separately (as I did).

sections.title: Social, Next Meeting 11th January
sections.content:
Happy New Year

Our first social evening of 2024 falls on the 11th of January (to allow us all to bed in a bit…). As always, we’re in the Horse and Farrier in Otley at 730pm for an 8pm start.

On this day, we are proud to present Mr Richard Meek regaling us with his exploits over the last year which brought him such fine accolades and fitting trophies of achievement. Richard is known not only for his fine flying abilities, but also for his expert films, photographs and storytelling attributes.

Not to be missed.

DHPC Film and Photo Night Feb 1st

And talking of films, photos and not to be missed, please submit your short films (no longer than 15 mins) and photographs (no bigger than 10MB) to me if you’d like to be in the running to win FANTASTIC prizes at our February social meet on Thursday the 1st of Feb.

Film first prize - Club T Shirt (or Annual membership of DHPC)

Photo first prize - Club T Shirt (or Annual membership of DHPC)

Other prizes, too, for second and third places.

Send films and photos to me by Jan 29th at 0900 to be in the running.

Next club nights are:

March 7th with Kacper and his BP Cup exploits

April 4th with Westie on how to win at Hike and Fly

sections.title: Flight Reports and Articles
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Cloud Cover in Oktas

A few months ago, somebody asked me why my messages to the WhatsApp Flying Plans Group gave my estimate of cloud cover in eighths – e.g. 6/8ths.  He’d wondered if he could use any fraction – say ⅓ or ¾.

The simple answer is that it’s the convention in meteorology to use Oktas (or eighths).  I’ve never questioned the logic, but I suppose that it’s easy, when looking up at the sky, to split it into quarters, and then one more split and you have Oktas.  It’s also convention to use improper fractions, so it’s 4/8ths (or 4 Oktas) rather than ½ cloud cover.

Oktas also play a part in decoding weather reports and forecasts. METARs (METeorological Aerodrome Report) and TAFs (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast) use text terms to describe the amount of cloud at various heights and the decode is as follows:

Text

Abbreviation

Amount in Oktas

Sky Clear

SKC

0

Few

FEW

1 – 2

Scattered

SCT

3 - 4

Broken

BKN

5 – 7

Overcast

OVC

8

For example, if the Leeds/Bradford METAR contained SCT023 BKN050 it would mean 3-4 Okts at 2,300’ and 5-7 Oktas at 5,000’ (both above airfield elevation).

If somebody reports 9 Oktas it means that they can’t observe the sky, normally because of fog or mist, but in my case it might be my duvet!

sections.title: Tales from a teabag; porosity matters
sections.content:
Pete D reports

It was an honest hike, but a pleasant one in the warm and picturesque surroundings of the Craig Meagaidh nature reserve – day 3 of our 2023 Scotland adventure.  Upon reaching Na Cnapanan it was noticeable that there was no wind, and what no wind there was had a distinctly northerly feel to it.  This meant that within a minute of stopping the midges found us, and to escape them we would be launching into the lee.

We hiked up further to the southwest faces of Càrn Lath and waited for the persistent cloud shading out launch to disperse.  A few got bored and gambled.  This even paid off in some cases, although not without an epic fight.  Eventually a midge-fart of a breeze came up the face and the half-dozen of us who were ready, or desperate to evade the irritating little invertebrates, threw ourselves off the hill.

It was a buoyant but snotty little mess of a lee-side thermal: difficult to stay in, too close to the terrain for comfort, and too many of us to relax.  Or was it just me?  I felt like I was accelerating and decelerating all the time as I went in and out of the core and having to put in large control movements to keep stable.  Eventually one of these was over the top and the tips peeled back as an anxious Robin flew past in the other direction.  Hands up, catch, straight line away from the mess, recover.  All fine in practice but a nagging feeling continued this time; something was not as it should be?

Two days prior we had been waiting on top of Creag Dubh above Glencoe ski resort with a light northeastly, looking for the sign.  It arrived in the form a cloud building about 500m from launch.  Dougie, Richard and Rosie, being the ones occupying the scant patches of dryish moss between the crags, piled off and I did a rapid half-hearted attempt at laying out my wing and chasing them.  As my feet left the ground, I looked up to see a perfect bowtie on the right side of the wing.  First reaction, fly out straight as the cliffs fell away beneath me; second, embarrassment at such a rushed imbecilic launch in front of another dozen pilots, both beginner and expert; third, danger over now how Pete’ll fix it?

Fifty seconds can be a very long time; ample time to confirm that the stabilo and brakes were not going to sort this mess, and enough to contemplate a bottom landing on the soft ground by the car park, followed by a pack, another £15 chair lift, and twenty-minute hike to take off.  No!  I was not going to lose an hour or more from a rare epic Scottish cross-country day, especially as the other three were now climbing ahead of me under the increasingly good-looking cloud.

I can’t remember consciously waiting until a specific height above ground, but the tracklog says precisely 200m so perhaps I was.  Once the cliffs had fallen away sufficiently, and this seemed to take an age with such a degraded glide angle, I tucked my legs in, braced and buried the righthand brake.  SIV training is all well and good when done over a lake with a world class instructor watching your every move; it turns out to be a slightly different kettle of fish over a remote Scottish mountainside with a mixed bag of bemused pilots wondering how you are going to get out of the fix.  I did however remember Malin’s words to the effect that spins are something that can be unpredictable; I did not want to have to repeat this so went hard on the first attempt.

The right-hand wing peeled back, and as I turned to face the cliffs I had recklessly departed from, I went to catch the dive.  A quick glance up suggested that most of the lineover had cleared, hurrah, and I was left with a little cravat.  However, the next thing I know I am spinning again, but this time unintentionally.  Hands up, let the wing dive properly, notice I am now flying away from the cliffs again and catch.  The remaining cravat was easily fixed by some line jiggling as my heart rate dropped towards normality and I headed out towards the cloud way below the others who were now well established in a climb.  It is at this point that I first heard the radio with someone on launch piping up with “…from zero to hero…”.  It turns out that there had been other, perhaps useful communications prior to this, such as trying a huge big-ear, but I guess I was too focussed.  Unlike two days later, I was able to put this first incident out of my head and continued on an epic flight across Blackwater dam to Loch Treig, back along the Mamores before making a valiant attempt to get back to Glencoe.

So why had my wing attempted to fall out the sky on me twice in three days when I was pretty current, had 450 hours on the wing, and had never previously accidentally stalled or gone parachutal on any wing?  The last piece of this puzzle, although not chronologically speaking, is that I had had a nagging feeling about the wing for a little while, nothing I could put my finger on, and so had popped into The Sick and the Wrong on the way to Scotland.  The numbers from Steve’s porosity meter did not make great reading; whilst the small white bit of central fabric was medium, the six readings on the red fabric either side were all “inappropriate” or fails with the lowest being 7 seconds.  I was advised not to fly it (https://porosimeter.hu/products/porozit-n/).

When the porosity deteriorates on wings it becomes easier for air to flow through the material.  This in turn disrupts the laminar flow on the wing surface, the thing that keeps it in the air, and the stall speed increases; the glider is more likely to enter deep stalls.  Whilst 450 hours should be fine on a modern well looked-after wing, I had purchased this one second hand in the first place so I would guess it had a minimum of another 100 hours on that.

So, there it was, in-flight verification of the porosity readings I was choosing to ignore, a condemned sail.  My advice: this is a good lesson to learn from other people’s experience as opposed to first hand!

sections.title: Members
sections.content:
Rahul Basu

It's time to start renewing your membership if you want to get the 10% discount . The club operates a fixed membership year and they expire on 1st February 2023. The good news is that the membership fees are staying the same as last year. So normally it's £25 but £22.50 with the 10% Discount for early renewal.

You will be emailed a renewal reminder which will contain a link to the Membership renew page. To renew please sign into your Membership, confirm your details and change where needed and submit your renewal form. You don't need a password (though you can create one if you prefer) and it only takes a minute or two. You will also need to make a payment, you are not renewed until you have both completed the renewal form and also paid the membership fee.

When making your payment separately please Remember to include your BHPA number as a payment reference. It makes it so much easier to reconcile payments with applications.

If you have set up a standing order to pay automatically, please check it is paid by 1st Feb.  You will still need to complete your membership application.

It is a condition of DHPC membership that you also remain a current member of the BHPA, so please remember to also renew your BHPA membership promptly when it becomes due.

I hope you choose to remain a member of the DHPC for another year. Best wishes for a safe and successful flying season!

sections.title: Safety
sections.content:
Clive Sury reports

Happy New Year!

Many people start the year with resolutions.  So why not have a few New Year paragliding ones?

Here are some ideas:

  • Download the what3words app so that you can share a location anywhere in the world in an emergency.
  • And/ or download the Ordnance Survey app for location and maps
  • Download the BHPA app to have your membership on your phone
  • Complete the Dales personal information record https://www.dhpc.org.uk/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2936 and send to coaching@dhpc.org.uk
  • Attend the Dales Club reserve repack on 14th Jan - register here: https://www.eventbrite.co.uk/e/dales-club-reserve-repack-day-sunday-14th-jan-2024-tickets-758968203327
  • Buy new Karabiners for your harness - noting a recent fatality in Turkey due to a broken Karabiner.  The recommendation is to replace them every 5 years or 500 hours, whichever first.
  • Set improvement goals on specific aspects of flying, e.g., spot landings; fully inflated glider at launch; forward launches; smoother and tighter 360’s
  • Improve core strength with a 2 minute plank each morning
  • Read a book every month or quarter
  • Attend a first aid and/ or coaching course
  • Perfect the Cobra launch technique
  • Upgrade equipment - the list here is endless!

These are just a few suggestions to get one’s mind engaged.  For a resolution to be worthwhile it’s important that it’s relevant to the individual concerned.  Plus, that the reward for achieving it is known and in front of mind.  Therefore, it’s what you consider important and wish to commit to yourself that matters.

The key point here is that this time of year provides an opportunity to reflect on what’s gone well with flying over the past year and to congratulate oneself for achievements made.  Thereafter, identify areas to improve or change and focus on going forward.  A great source of joy in the sport is the continual progression in ability and knowledge that can be achieved and I wish you well in your development over 2024.

Safe flying,

Clive.

sections.title: Northern Challenge Series (NCS) -  2024
sections.content:
Ed Cleasby updates.

(This is written principally for newer pilots who may be unfamiliar with the NCS)

The 7th year of the Northern Challenge Series (NCS) kicks off on the 1st February.  The 2024 series has been substantially refreshed with new tasks and several new sites including a revised approach to the Hike & Fly set.  I’ll  run through the changes later, but for pilots new to the concept of the NCS it may be useful to briefly revisit the history and rationale underpinning it.

Some background to the NCS

The main purpose of the NCS is pilot development, however,  it also serves as a competition if you wish to approach it that way. It includes a league table with a scoring system with awards given out at year end.  It emerged from my time as Chief Coach, when I realised that coaching days were often hard to coordinate with the weather; they needed a focus to tie in with individual pilot needs and the primary benefit for new pilots  often became networking – getting to know faces and become flying buddies.  The ‘focus’ aspect I addressed using task cards set at differing levels, later that developed into the standing tasks/challenges that enlarged to form the NCS.  My answer to the weather and the ‘herding cats’ problem,  was to make tasks available and accessible across an extended season.  In other words they can be attempted whenever convenience and the weather coincides. The means to host everything is via  the excellent, xcmap, run by Chris Foster. This provides all the task uploads and flight downloads  If you’re in the first few years of your flying career the NCS offers a pathway to accelerated learning and dovetails easily into coaching or self directed activities.   At the other end of the experience spectrum it provides a racing element – in other words -  against the clock.

Why is the NCS good for pilot development?

First of all, there is nothing wrong with wafting around for an hour enjoying the sensation and the scenery.  I have  many years of doing just that– and still do.  But… you won’t learn a great deal – about yourself, your wing, the site, conditions, or that app/instrument on your flightdeck.  Flying any NCS task will challenge you to push the envelope of all those things whilst increasing your knowledge and skills. The tasks are all site based and designed to  increase in difficulty through to the satisfying  ping of achieving goal (60%).  In terms of difficulty they are of modest length and suitable for the average pilot – the time element (40%)  is for the more ambitious and competitive pilots who wish to race them.  In summary

i)                    You develop navigation skills through setting up and flying a task.

ii)                   You gain a deeper understanding of what your app/instrument is capable of and how to set it up.

iii)                 You explore the extended possibilities of a site instead of staying within your comfort zone beats..

iv)                 You push your own flying skills in order to progress through a series of turnpoints.

v)                  Being focused helps avoid negative thoughts and keeps you in a positive frame of mind.

vi)                 It’s both rewarding and fun.

For more details of the Aims, rules, tasks, scoring etc, go to https://www.xcflight.com/northern-challenge-series-2024/   At the time of writing the tasks are set as illustrative downloads only, they should be live for download on  https://www.xcmap.net/   after the New Year.

Changes for 2024

Main series:  There is no change to the number of tasks, which is held at 14 (best eight scoring).  There are 9 new tasks and four new sites. All the remaining GRIDS have gone, I admit to not liking GRID tasks!  However, no GRID or previous task is lost, they have simply reverted back into the relevant club sets. A few of the new tasks are tweaked originals to make them a little easier – others are completely new and designed to get pilots to explore less well flown sites. An example (left) is Nappa, which having flown there a few times over the past two years I feel deserves more attention.

Another slight change of emphasis is to move tasks away from areas where they weren’t seeing any traffic.  Another consideration is if a club supports and encourages the NCS they get tasks - if not they lose them!  

Hike & Fly series:  This remains at six tasks with the best four scoring. To be more inclusive (less daunting) they have been reduced in length and only the ‘iconic’ Dales Three Peaks remains from the previous set. They can now be regarded as ‘day tasks’.  The spread across the Lakes, Dales and Pennines is about equal. Given a reasonable day it’s quite possible to fly over 50%  of a task as they are based around ridges, bowls and good thermal territory  and allow for different wind directions. An interesting example is the Haweswater circuit in the eastern Lakes (right).

In addition to the Dales Three Peaks, DHPC pilots will find they have two new, local H&F tasks to whet their appetite. They may prove useful training for the  X Dales later in the year.

That is a summary of the NCS 2024.  I don’t need to add anything else as everything is covered via the previous links.  

Any questions please direct to me.

Get planning …. it’s FREE!

—--------------------------------------------------------------------

The 2023 awards were recently presented at the J36 Pizza night as follows.

Main series - Geoff Crossley        Hike & Fly   - Tom Hodgkin        Highest placed ENA/B - Elliott Brown

sections.title: All Our Yesterdays
sections.content:
If you feel the past should be left, quietly swallowed by the mists of time,  then the  article below is not for you - move on.

However, it shows nothing really changes but the names and the gliders - the experience remains the same, the excitement undiminished, the challenge no different …as for the weather I couldn’t possibly comment.

Back in the day when Wings! produced lots of great local articles, the Dales and Lakes were favoured with some many, first encounters with wave, convergence and thermalling xc flights. I’m fortunate to have most of the very early copies dating back into the late seventies. I still enjoy reading them …. partly nostalgia, but also because they still have a raw freshness and still seem relevant today.

Wings!

May 1980

(Apologies for the reproduction quality, you may need to zoom in and put on your best glasses).

Ed Cleasby

Martin Baxter adds…

Great minds think alike.  In a remarkable coincidence (or is it just that the bad weather is keeping us indoors?), I have also been digging through the archives.

As you may have read on the forum, Lee Collinson offered up some old magazines.  Marc Asquith agreed to take them and, in a logistical operation of which Eddie Stobart would be proud, Lee (whilst visiting his brother) delivered three boxes to Pete Logan in Baildon; Pete handed them to me at the club AGM; and I plan to give them to Marc at the BHPA AGM.  No idea what Marc does with them all.

I couldn’t help but flick through a few of the older copies and, to my surprise, I found them quite illuminating.  I scanned a few items that I thought might be of interest to club members, and I’ll include them in future copies of Skywords (if Ed doesn’t beat me to it!).

I found some of the history quite illuminating.  Prior to 1989 the British Hang Gliding Association (BHGA) had its own magazine called ‘Wings!’ and the British Association of Parascending Clubs (BAPC) had a magazine called ‘Skywalker’.  These magazines were merged into ‘Skywings’ in Apr/May 1989.  Despite resistance from some BHGA members about the new fangled sport of paragliding: mostly concerned about safety and the dangers of mixed flying at congested sites, the associations merged to form the BHPA in 1992.

Flicking through some of the old copies of Wings, it’s clear that DHPC members played a significant role in the BHGA, and the evolution of our sport.  Noel Whittall was chairman of the BHGA.  His son, Robbie, became World Champion in hang gliding, and later in paragliding.  Both are honorary club members.  Trevor Birkbeck, Dean Crosby, Ian Currer and Ed Cleasby all played their part in developing the sport into what it is today.

From Skywalker January 1987:                                        and one month later:
